data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Bullish Sentiment Jumps
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, both neutral sentiment and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 7.0 percentage points to 49.5%. Bullish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 37.5% for the seventh time in 10 weeks. Optimism was last higher on November 14, 2024 (49.8%).

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 5.2 percentage points to 22.3%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 78th time in 80 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 1.8 percentage points to 28.2%. Bearish sentiment is below its historical average of 31.0% for the fifth time in seven weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 8.8 percentage points to 21.3%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the 11th time in 50 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how their portfolio performed in 2025 relative to their expectations.

Here is how they responded:

  • Much better than I expected: 21.3%.
  • Better than I expected: 46.6%.
  • Close to how I expected: 20.2%.
  • Worse than I expected: 9.1%.
  • Much worse than I expected: 2.0%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 49.5%, up 7.0 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 22.3%, down 5.2 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 28.2%, down 1.8 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: Gambling.Com Group: Dirt Cheap Pick-And-Shovel Gambling Growth Play
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Liam Tormey as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

The best investment I’m aware of today is a highly profitable, founder-led, beaten down businesses that operates within the Gambling industry without the regulatory overhangs. Gambling.com Group Limited (GAMB) is an affiliate marketing business rapidly transitioning to sports data services and consistently growing reliable recurring revenue.

Stated plainly, Gambling.com sends customers to online casinos and sportsbooks on commission and now sells odds data directly to bettors and sportsbooks.

In other words they run a slowing high margin core business segment and a rapidly growing recently acquired tangential business. The cash these segments kick off is so substantial relative to the current share price and growth prospects that it’s positively preposterous.

Business Overview and Prospects (Does a Durable competitive advantage exist?)

At the end of 2025 the Total Enterprise Value/Unlevered Free Cash Flow of Gambling.com sat at 4.5x a far cry from 25.8x multiple they traded at December 31st 2021, the year of their IPO. EBITDA in the first 9 months of 2025 has grown to $32.13 million from $12.53 million the same period 4 years ago. Ignoring contingent considerations (~$79.7 million), the TEV at the end of 2025 is $265 million, a fair bit below what they were valued at by year end 2021 which was $297.6 million. Including the discounted fair value of these contingent considerations the enterprise value has increased 15.8% to $344.7 million while EBITDA increased 156% and FY 2025 revenue is projected to end the year up 290% from FY 2021.

A more accurate comparison to Gambling.com’s cohorts above shows their depressed valuation. Better Collective A/S (BTRCF) is their nearest publicly traded competitor on the affiliate side of the business. Like Gambling.com Group, Better Collective has grown through acquisitions but focus on direct durable traffic through destination news sites for games like FIFA and Counter Strike, two communities notorious for in and out of game gambling. As a result Better Collective is less impacted by short term updates or issues with Google search but maintains lower margins than Gambling.com.

Sportradar Group AG (SRAD) and Genius Sports Limited (GENI) are leaders in the sports data business with exclusive league partnerships with NHL, MLB, and NFL. Sportradar Group is the largest of the three offering broad sports statistics and primary sports data feeds whereas Gambling.com, through its OpticOdds and OddsJam acquisition, aims to be the fastest odds aggregator quickly comparing data between sportsbooks.

The core affiliate and advertising segment of Gambling.com’s business accounted for 76% of its revenue in Q3 ($29.8 million) and the data services segment is a rapidly growing 24% ($9.19 million) that could surpass their core segment in top line revenue as early as 1H 2027. Before 2025 the data services segment was primarily Rotowire, their B2C fantasy sports subscription service, so the segment experienced explosive growth as a result of its Odds Holding acquisition in 2025 recording 304% YOY revenue growth in Q3 2025. OpticOdds in particular experienced 100% YOY revenue growth in Q3, this segment contains the longest term contracts from sportsbooks, prediction markets, market makers, and media who buy their odds data and related services which improves the reliability of Gambling.com’s revenue as it grows.

“Given the long runway we have for consistent growth in our sports data services business, we believe that this exciting future will be the core of GAMB” —Charles Gillespie (Q3 2025 earnings call)

Gambling.com has long run affiliate marketing across more than 50 high authority websites cost-effectively connecting gamblers to second and third tier online casinos and sportsbooks at gross margins well above 90%. Fixed costs for that segment amount to a few bucks per year on domain renewals and hosting fees. The Data Analytics segments spends a few million a year on compute for their high frequency data aggregation and dissemination so gross profit margin takes a hit but the operating margins are still within range of the healthy high margin affiliate business.

They have grown that revenue and profit rapidly, revenue is up over 900% over 7 years, while maintaining high margins since they require no capital intensive physical assets and avoid the regulatory risk inherent in almost every other corner of this industry. Yet their P/S and EV/FCF multiples continue to compress in complete contrast to reality. The business is better than ever and the team has prudently reduced their dependence on google search by expanding their non-SEO affiliate revenue across email, social media, and revenue share partnerships with sites like USA TODAY where Gambling.com handles all their on site affiliate gambling advertising.

“In Q4, we expect to generate more revenue from non-SEO channels than SEO for the first time as a public company.”—Charles Gillespie (Q3 Earnings Call)

The other half of this diversification effort has been three acquisitions, most recently Spotlight.vegas, Roto Sports, and Odds Holdings (OpticOdds & OddsJam). I’ll go in depth on the others in the future but for brevity we will focus on the most fruitful so far, OpticOdds. This is their B2B low latency sports odds data business which is expanding beyond odds data into an all in one platform solution offering bet acceptance, settlement, and risk management to sports betting platforms. The Jan 1st 2025 acquisition was structured as $80 million upfront and $80 million in performance based earn-outs and after drastically growing revenue this year management expects to happily pay those earn outs.

“OddsJam and OpticOdds are doing really well. They are in a good position to capture most, if not all, of the contingent consideration in respect of 2025. That means that we will owe them $40 million in April ‘26 and $20 million in April ‘27” —Charles Gillespie (Q3 Earnings Call)

This re-evaluation of the earn out likelihood incurred a non-cash accounting expense which when combined with flat revenue from their core business likely spooked some investors when combined with the GAAP net loss reported in the third quarter. Whatever the case the stock price took a 23% single day dive dropping from $6.84 to $5.25 per share. It went on to reach its 52-Week low of $4.60 a week later. An odd reaction to record revenue, and a rare opportunity to invest in a healthy growing company for $180 million the very same year that they made a homerun acquisition for a much smaller healthy growing company for $160 million. In fact when you include Spotlight Vegas, the value of Gambling.com Group’s two most recent acquisitions are now greater than the combined companies current market cap. The Spotlight.Vegas deal closed September 1st for $8 million in cash and $22 million in performance based earn-outs for a total of $30 million making the cumulative potential acquisition cost of the two businesses $190 million.

Valuation: Expectations and Reality

As we close out the year management expects to end Q4 2025 with a record $46 million in revenue. If we take the midpoint of their 33-34% Q4 margin guidance that implies EBITDA of $15.41 million. This would bring FY 2025 revenue to $165.2 million with $51.7 million in EBITDA (Up from $127 million revenue and $41.4 million EBITDA 2024). In a recent interview the CEO hinted at outperformance of these Q4 numbers as a result of strong performance from Missouri’s online sportsbook legalization in November.

“If we turn into 2026, we expect to see overall revenue growth in the low-teens…in Q3, our EBITDA margin was 33%...Our Q4 guidance looks towards 33%, 34%. I think that’s pretty indicative for our expectations for 2026.” -Elias Mark CFO (Q3 2025 Earnings Call)

Next year management expects revenue growth of ~14% and EBITDA margins of ~33.5% which represents a 7.4% relative margin expansion and an absolute increase of 2.3% from 2025. That implies $188.3 million/$63 million of 2026 revenue and EBITDA respectively. If their $51.7 million 2025 EBITDA number is correct that would give us 21.8% EBITDA growth next year, far cry from a business in decline.

Based on historical growth trends management appears to have set beatable targets for Q4 and FY 2026, CEO Gillespie spoke to that effect in an interview with StockTwits where he shared his frustration with the market reaction to earnings this year. He also suggests they are on pace to beat those Q4 targets. Management does not include new state legalizations in their estimates until they are live, as a result Missouri's OSB legalization was not accounted for in these EBITDA and Revenue figures. The likelihood of new state legalizations, while low, is greater than zero. Steven Pizzella of Deutsche Bank believes that Maryland has the best shot in legalizing online casinos in 2026. Regardless of what happens in 2026, Missouri should prove an ongoing tailwind, likely having added north of a million in revenue in December 2025 and millions more in 2026.

The company bought back 562,222 shares at an average price of $8.33 in Q3 and they have the buyback budget ($14.4 million) and the trading volume to allow them to exhaust that budget and repurchase five times as many shares in Q4 if they choose.

Growth through acquisition is rarely a winning strategy, just ask NNDM former CEO, but CEO Charles Gillespie and his team seem to have hit a homerun with Odds Holding. They now have recurring revenue growing rapidly and despite a recent stumble their core business has been no slouch itself. Organic growth in the affiliate marketing business averaged 50% from 2017 to 2024. Most recently falling from 41% in 2023 to 2% in 2024.

By 2027 the Odds Holding acquisition will have cost $168.7 million in cash and $10 million in shares. Assuming its data services business accounts for one third of Revenue and EBITDA in 2026 ($62.8million/ $21million) they will have paid just under 3 times earnings and 8.5x EBITDA - not a paltry sum and a higher premium than they typically pay for acquisitions. However it should continue to be very fruitful considering the current hyper growth rate of that business and the runway for continued expansion into OSB's where GAMB has long established relationships. They are now the primary sports odds data provider for Kalshi and Polymarket and have established themselves as a market leader in a segment poised for further double digit growth. These event contracts, while closer to options trading, still require the starting line setting and bet settlement that traditional sportsbooks use which OpticOdds specialize in. Platforms like Robinhood markets, Inc. (HOOD) who currently use Kalshi and in turn use Gambling.com's OpticOdds are looking to build their own prediction market offering and as more platforms like Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) follow suit OpticOdds is poised to add more long term lucrative clients.

They have reasonable debt (1.8 LTM Debt/EBITDA or 3.7x when including all contingent considerations) and multiple binary potential state legalization outcomes likely to occur in the next 18 months all of which are not priced into their guidance including Missouri which has been live since December 1st.

All told Gambling.com has sextupled EBITDA and quadrupled revenue since 2021 but despite it all the share price highs of 2021 were three times what the much improved business trades at today.

Inflection Point: What may move the multiple?

There aren’t many healthy companies with a market cap less than three times management’s EBITDA guidance for next year but here we are.

Buybacks are underway but the cash on hand is a bit sparse so buybacks won’t be an enormous lever. The Swish lawsuit will play out in 2026. Any news of a settlement, win, or dismissal will move the stock price. Even a partial loss may be viewed positively as it removes the overhang of uncertainty that investors hate.

The coming state legalizations are easy to understand growth catalysts as America is Gambling.com Groups largest single market. In 2024 US, Mexico, and Canada accounted for $55 million of their total $127 million in operating revenue. Ireland and the UK brought in $39 million. Americas dominance on their income statement only grew in 2025 and this is notable since online gambling was only legalized in the US in 2018, whereas Gambling.com formerly KAX media has had a 16 year presence in the UK/Ireland.

As the full breadth of American degeneracy is unlocked state by state we will see spikes in revenue and eventually a spike in that valuation multiple. But for a long term shareholder the longer valuation remains disconnected and depressed the longer Gambling.com can cheaply buy back shares, increasing our stake, and cheaply and tax efficiently compounding our EPS growth. The only time depressed stock prices are a bad thing is when you’re holding a bad business that you want out of or the SBC outpaces share repurchasing.

They need to be disciplined with capital allocation and pay down debt as the $38.7 million in contingent considerations they just paid early will bring the 2025 Q4 net debt to ~$111.9 million and the additional $40 million in contingent considerations due in 15 months will need to be paid in cash if stock prices remain depressed. I'll be watching net debt in 2026, any extension or favorable refinancing of their debt should do well to increase investor confidence.

The most likely catalyst to move investor sentiment is simply consistent guidance beats from continued quarterly growth. It’s a small cap sin stock but it's well covered for its size and as the attractive SAAS data business outpaces the boring affiliate business someone at a bank will report the obvious long after it's become obvious.

Management Ability and Ethics

A good quick litmus test for ethics, alignment, and the health of free cash flow is how aggressively management uses stock based compensation relative to their revenue and free cash flow.

Since their IPO the value of their yearly SBC has increased 3.5x. While significant, stock based compensation still being outpaced by free cash flow growth which in 2025 is 4x what it was in 2021.

We can see stock based compensation is a factor worth watching sitting at around 4% of revenue. Since their IPO the company has generated $49.4 million in FCF of which they used a sizable $18.3 million for SBC. Roughly 37% of their total free cash flow has gone to employees, YTD that percentage of FCF flowing through to SBC has dropped to 21% which you would hope continues to drop as they grow.

Looking at dilution as a whole we can see share count is relatively stable for a small young company as they rarely issue shares as a part of their acquisitions, using only $8 million worth of shares in their $180 million Odds Holding Acquisition. As I finalize this article on December 30th their CEO announced that of the remaining $60 million in Odds Holding earn outs they have agreed to pay $40 million early in all cash (saving themselves about $1.3 million in early payment).

This reaffirms my earlier point and also adds a potential stock price tailwind, the former Odds Holding owners might like to own a larger piece of their new combined business especially at these depressed prices and they were just handed $38,700,000 dollars by which to do it.

Insider ownership as it stands today shows CEO Charles Gillespie and his co-founder COO Mark Blandford maintain healthy large stakes in the business. Charles Gillespie has been slowly reducing his stake since the IPO but recently raised his stake for the first time to just over 11%.

From what we’ve seen of his acquisitions play out, pace of historical growth, and what he’s said throughout 18 earnings calls I like him and his team. Talent as an operator is always tougher to quantify but his talent in capital allocation has been pretty darn good.

Prediction Markets, Risks, and Unknowns

To accurately assess a business it needs to be simple enough to understand, or you need to be sophisticated enough to understand complicated businesses. I can’t differentiate a soup spoon from a ladle so I stick with simple businesses and although ever expanding Gambling.com Group remains simple enough. The areas we need to explore deeper to get a full appreciation for the risks and strategic position of Gambling.com’s business are five fold.

We need to understand the health of the sportsbook and casino middle class. Gambling.com makes most their money with tier 2 and tier 3 operators and a single Casino/OSB ruling all is bad for business. On the other side of the business we must assess the competitive landscape of the B2B odds platforms. We need to quantify the risks from the fickle google algorithm and search rankings. Along those lines we need to determine the financial risk AI poses to search as we know it and how quickly it could reduce future affiliate revenue in a worst case scenario.

Furthermore it's important to understand where the industries of gambling and sports betting are going/growing and the impact of prediction markets within the US and abroad. Lastly we need to evaluate their exposure in the Swish lawsuit, the likelihood of future lawsuits, and subsequent repercussions on the long term viability of their SAAS business that a temporary or permanent loss or delay of access to Swish Analytics odds would bring.

I won’t cover it all here today but let’s examine the most impactful; AI and the meteoric rise of prediction markets, but first an uninformed comment on the lawsuit from a community college dropout.

Swish Analytics is a smaller private company that is the primary odds provider for Bet365, FanDuel (DUEL), and DraftKings Inc. (DKNG). By headcount they are approximately a quarter of the size of Gambling.com and their case seems to have some validity as the first motion to dismiss was rejected at the beginning of the month.

I believe Gambling.com has insulation from potential financial penalties of the Swish lawsuit as it was filed exactly a day before they acquired it. The Right of Set-Off allows any lawsuits pre buyout to be covered by future earn outs owed to the founders of Odds Holding. Whether management expects to be indemnified from any financial penalties is not clear, they are certainly still paying lawyers fees and exposed to the more foundational risk that they get cut off from fanduel and bet365 analytics. If the case goes forward and they lose, they likely need to pay up to use the data or get locked out entirely, partially crippling some of OpticOdds offering and to a lesser degree OddsJam.

This is all speculation, I haven’t come across anything concrete outlining the specifics of their acquisition terms sheet or read any illuminating comments from management.

Much of this 63% YTD decline in share price is owed to fear surrounding the expected AI assisted suicide of their affiliate search business. AI may kill search as we know it one day but a quick look at Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG) dsearch revenue show’s us it is not this day.

The plain reality is google search remains strong and they are incentivized more than anyone to maintain its integrity while integrating it further into Google Gemini.

Uncertainty around Gambling.com’s place in this AI future, in part, is what led to this value investing opportunity. AI and the rapid rise of prediction markets threaten to reshape the landscape of both their businesses. When potential customers ask Gemini or Chat GPT directly for sport book bonus promotions gambling.com group's army of 50+ websites are reduced to, at best, a cited source. Prediction markets in particular are expanding aggressively into sports betting where it offers systemically superior margins. Although regulation is still in flux, it is plausible that Polymarket and Kalshi will remain available in states and to age groups (18-20) where sports betting is still illegal. The market doesn’t like uncertainty, but there’s good reason to expect this prediction market growth while cannibalistic to existing sportsbooks will be additive to the overall growth of the industry bringing new customers that Gambling.com can continue to profitably funnel to a changing landscape of operators.

The risk for Gambling.com is that prediction markets inherently improve with more users and greater liquidity which may lead to a winner takes all situation. By contrast FanDuel and DraftKings together command around 60% of the sportsbook market in the US which leaves a large healthy market of second tier and smaller niche operators which are Gambling.com Group’s bread and butter. Small sportsbooks can’t afford to spend the millions per year in compute required for up to date odds and bet settlement, OpticOdds offers this to them at a fraction of the price.

While sportsbooks are regulated by state Gaming Commissions the prediction markets fall under the purview of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission and are regulated at the federal level. As it stands in the US 19 states have yet to legalize online sports betting most notably California, Texas, and Georgia. Those 19 states account for 37% of the US population, a 124,000,000 untapped market that prediction markets are unlocking. If Gambling.com can integrate themselves deeper with prediction markets through both the customer funnel portion of their business and the odds data services business, this potential risk becomes a huge tailwind.

Where things get murky for Gambling.com is in their ability to sell OpticOdds services to prediction market makers. Prediction market odds are self setting, for every winner there is an equal loser. A zero sum game where the probability becomes the payout doesn’t require risk mitigation services the way a traditional sportsbook operator needs to balance exposure to guarantee profit. They only really need binary bet settlement and the starting point for the original line.

Timely bet settlement as it relates to sports betting is where OpticOdds has a use case. This service could be further expanded if prediction markets get deeper into live sports micro market betting. As it stands OpticOdds is the primary sports data partner for both Kalshi and Polymarket. They are also used by Robinhood and indirectly by Coinbase, both of whom use Kalshi under the hood for their prediction market offerings. Gambling.com is perhaps best positioned to profit from prediction markets through their traditional affiliate marketing, media partnership advertising, and public facing OddsJam subscriptions. It seems the market is missing the explosive growth opportunity of prediction markets that GAMB is just starting to capture.

Valuation

Management is guiding for 14% revenue growth in 2026 and 2.3% absolute growth in EBITDA margins for a double digits growth of adjusted EBITDA next year.

Accounting for SBC the adjusted EBITDA since the 2021 IPO has grown from $16.4 million to an expected $44.7 million in 2025 — that’s a CAGR of 28.5%. Next year’s conservative management estimate of $63 million in adjusted EBITDA (after subtracting $8 in SBC) would put us at another year of 23% growth.

My base case DCF above assumes 2026 revenue and margins mildly underperform management’s expectations and then averages 12.5% growth thereafter as operating margins stabilize below historical levels. Assuming a modest terminal multiple the stock is a 7-bagger with a fair value today of $19.73. An EV/EBIT multiple of 12 is squarely where GAMB's historically closest competitor Better Collective A/S (BTRCF) has been valued for the last 2 years. In a bull case there's a path to GAMB one day earning a SAAS premium valuation more akin to S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) which currently has an EV/EBIT multiple of 20.8x.

When multi-year B2B contracts from OpticOdds begin to dominate the income statement the reliability of those returns make calculating the future value of GAMB cash flows much easier. The margin of safety here is so great that either of their business segments could fail completely in 2026 and the remaining segment would likely still justify today’s enterprise value (even adding the $80MM+ in contingent considerations). It's impossible to say where the stock price will trade in the next 6-12 months as price is dependent on the mood and rationality of the market. Be mindful of the inflection points mentioned above. Keep an eye on online casino legislation particularly in New York and Illinois, and online sports betting legalization in California, Georgia, and Minnesota. State legalization is not included in company guidance and any additions are gravy to GAMB's bottomline.

I would be surprised if Gambling.com Group ends 2026 south of $7.75 a share. Assuming Q4 EBITDA hits $16.3 million, less $1.4 million in nterest, $3.4 million Tax and $0.5 million CapEx, that leaves $11 million of levered free cash flow for the quarter. If we add that to a conservative LFCF target of $48 million in 2025 we have $59 million by which to reduce net debt in 2026. Net debt will have grown $38.7 million due to the unreported early repayment of the 2026 OddsHolding earn-out. That leaves Gambling.com fwd$20.3 million by which to pay down debt or repurchase shares, all else equal it would be a 7.6% reduction in EV to $244.7 million. Given 2027 analyst EBITDA estimates of $70.38 million that would make for a NTM EV/EBITDA multiple of 3.48x at the end of 2026 down from 4.28x at the end of 2025. This implies a 23% appreciation in share price to $6.45 is required by the end of 2026 just to maintain the current depressed valuation. A valuation that is far off their 4-year EV/EBITDA average of 9.59x and further still from Better Collective average of 10.87x and SportsRadar Group average of 20.02x. I believe the addition of durable high margin SAAS revenue to the business should eventually earn them a multiple between BETCO and SRAD of 11.5x EV/EBITDA but I don't think we can expect the full 168% of margin expansion within a year. I do think we can safely expect a minimum of 20% margin expansion to 5.14x which together with the growth in earnings and reduction in debt as laid out would put us at $7.75 a share.

Conclusion

Often in value investing we must find mispricings by looking at debt-laden declining businesses or businesses otherwise encumbered by some combination of misfortune or managerial shortcoming — the longer I looked into Gambling.com the more I liked and the farther the stock price fell. We are looking at a business with consistent double digits revenue/FCF growth and a low single digits valuation that has multiple regulatory tailwinds, an aligned management, is capital light, has reasonable debt, 49% durable recurring revenue, fruitful acquisitions, is in a growing industry and its trading as though the business is dying while it’s rapidly growing, de-risked, and diversified.

A single year of slow down in their core affiliate business (from a google search update that erroneously prioritized spam content overseas) does not justify a valuation multiple under 4x FCF when the company as a whole is maintaining strong growth and guiding for more strong growth. Operating expenses need to be monitored as the company now manages ever higher headcounts across two distinct business segments. Despite these challenges it should be trading at a premium and the $14.4 million in share repurchases coming down the pipe will be a welcomed boon to my EPS in the coming years as top and bottom-line growth continues. It’s a tragedy they don’t have more dry powder to use on buybacks but as it stands they will end 2025 with roughly $111.9 million in net debt. In addition to that they have a discounted fair value of non-current contingent considerations from Q3 2025 of $16.1 million which will be raised in Q4 closer to $42 million to reflect the discounted fair value of the final $40 million in contingent considerations owed for Odds Holding on April 1st 2027 as well as a max of $22 million owed for Spotlight.Vegas, half due April 1st 2027 and the second half due a year later. This definitely limits buybacks and acquisitions in 2026 but is not yet a concern considering FCF is north of $40 million a year, they have over $50 million in undrawn credit, and they remain under their wells fargo leverage covenant of 3:1.

I like the risk reward here. The death of search has been exaggerated - the major acquisitions have been fairly priced and have set them up with a hand in every pot: gambling, sportsbooks, prediction markets, and live entertainment all while avoiding the regulatory risks inherent in those businesses.

data.title: Carl Zeiss Meditec: Why This Company Is A Good Choice For 2026
data.description:
Some investments that I make take a long time to mature. Some even take years - and if you're a value investor like I am, you have to kind of decide whether you'll allow companies to take as long as that to "mature". I argue that if you're unwilling to take this time, you should probably consider not using a value approach for your investments. It simply involves too much risk. The company I'm going to review is a perfect example of a business that has taken a very long time to mature (and is still far from there), but where the quality isn't anything that I am worried about in terms of the investment.

My last article on the company can be found here - a negative development of about 4% compared to a positive from the SPY of about 6%. Not a huge difference, but if you look at the longer-term performance difference, there's a significant variation with the company significantly underperforming indices.

This is therefore a position that is in the negative, and where I believe it will take some time to see recovery. Does that worry me?

Not really.

The reason why it doesn't bother or worry me as much as perhaps some other companies do is because the underlying fundamentals for the company are very solid. Recent results, which I will go into here, confirm this, and we are therefore seeing a negative correlation, i.e., the company is going down despite results going up. Whenever this happens, that can be a very good indicator of some interesting valuation potentials - and is why I'm excited to see the valuation at this time, even more so than before.

We're in a very inflated market overall. It's not wrong to characterize the market as 'overvalued', something I have done in previous pieces. I believe a company like this one is poised to outperform over the medium to longer term, provided that these positive trends continue.

Let's see what we have here.

Carl Zeiss Meditec: Recent Results Confirm My Upside And Considerations

The company's recent results are about a month old at this time, but I am surprised that we haven't seen more of a positive reaction as a result of them than we have here. Why?

Because Carl Zeiss Meditec achieved over €2.2B worth of revenue, an increase of over 7.5% YoY in this still volatile market, even higher at a constant currency and adjusted for M&A effects. EBITA was up as well - in line with the company's forecast and guidance, and the order backlog amounted to well over €350M, which is high for the company.

More importantly, Carl Zeiss noted a significant increase in order flows and market recovery.

Perhaps I would be less positive if the company's operations and business was concerning some less specialized, more commoditized sector. But this is not the case. The company is in a highly narrow segment, and owns some of the most attractive technologies and patents in that segment. As you can see above, the company has specialized to covert the entire lifecycle for its main treatment area, and due to increased average ages, we're likely to see increases in procedures.

The company's secondary segment, Microsurgery, is about 23% of total revenue for the full year, and is still growing. Furthermore, remember that almost 60% of shares are held not by market or float, but by Carl Zeiss AG, which makes foreign control or influence of this company, as of this time, a non-issue. No one on the broader market can affect what the company does, because a majority shareholding is private.

The company reached multiple milestones as of the full-year results. This includes a recurring revenue of 50% or above, which means that the company is now more and more insulated from its unit sales. This is a strong indicator that the company's earnings/AEPS should be treated with perhaps a bit more adherence to the historical premium, because forward volatility is likely to be lower. Higher percentages of service revenue tend to have that effect, after all.

The company's risks that remain are related to exposure - too much of the company's business is still in China at 25%, which has been a drag on revenues and profits, and a source of the volatility that has been driving this company down. At the same time, other APAC geographies are going well.

I, personally, believe that the company's prospects for China are bleak. With a weaker consumer climate and higher economic uncertainty in China, I find it unlikely that consumers (or commercial players) will show the same interest in the company's SMILE products and machines, which, while different than LASIK and offering higher margins, are also significantly more expensive. I would consider any forecast for the company based on a full China recovery as too exuberant.

Basically, a positive view on Carl Zeiss as of the end of the year is possible if you believe in the megatrends mentioned, in new product appeal, in new market sales and better margins (ex-China), and with good integration of companies like DORC, which is already contributing to revenues and results. I do not think it's wrong to say that competitors are less prepared than Carl Zeiss. None of them, Topcon, B+L, Alcon, or Haag-Streit, offer the breadth of services, such as diagnostics, imaging, refractive lasers, or vitrectomies, that this company does. Carl Zeiss is the only one with full vertical integration - and vertical integration is, in my view, a source of competitiveness and strength, as we saw, among other things, during COVID-19, which has been researched into since.

So, overall, I do not see why the company should trade negatively, or as cheaply as it does today. The current valuation puts Carl Zeiss at 23.8x P/E on average, but this needs to be put into the context of a 10-15 year history, which puts the company closer to 40x P/E. I have said in previous articles that this is one of the companies I allow some of the most premium in, and I stand by that.

Given the current growth estimates, even if they turn out 10-20% lower than estimated, the upside for this company is non-trivial.

Valuation For Carl Zeiss Meditec: I'm Now Buying More

Historically, it's often been between Carl Zeiss Meditec and peers like Healthineers (SEMHF). However, as of January 2026, I believe the advantage is skewed towards Carl Zeiss.

Even just a forecast to 30x P/E, which I have historically used as a bit of an indicator (and which is also the 20-year average, by the way), gives us an upside in the triple digits until 2028E.

Even discounted further, say to 20-21x P/E, lower than I believe this company has any right to be, the relative upside for the company is significant and worth considering. The one disadvantage for the business is the very low dividend yield. At this particular time, it's no more than 1.3%, despite the low valuation, and this means that your dividend returns or amounts are never really going to amount to (comparatively) much here.

Instead, focus on capital appreciation for the company, and focus on what you can get that way. An argument for Siemens Healthineers above Carl Zeiss Meditec is a higher yield, solid credit rating, and a more stable overall potential upside - but by comparison, Siemens Healthineers has an upside that's about 8-10% lower per year than Carl Zeiss.

My arguments for investing in this company remain. The company is a market leader with a moat, I believe is second to very few companies. It has a focus on innovation, proven by history. It has a strong financial situation, with extremely low overall debt, and above this has a very strong valuation and upside. I would say it's fair to characterize the company as "cheap" here, which means that it fulfills all of my investment criteria for 2026E.

It's also why I have decided to expand my position in Carl Zeiss Meditec, and why it's my number #1 pick in the sector as it stands today. I believe what headwinds there are, are mostly short-term in nature, and while the Chinese market is tricky, the risk I would instead focus on is geopolitical volatility - meaning tariffs.

But I believe even those will only take "so much" from the company.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risk to Carl Zeiss is a lengthened price/mix or market issue, as the company has been experiencing on and off for some time here. If this were to extend to longer periods, there would come a point where I would have to impact the company's fair value. As I've stated before, companies growing at much lower rates should not be valued at 30x+ P/E, especially not with a sub-2% yield. But that's not where we are; Carl Zeiss is instead a very strong grower of its revenues and earnings.

Secondary and tertiary risks (the ones that I would focus on here) are more related to tariffs. Given the company's large amount of exposure to NA and the current tariff situation (even though at the time of writing, the existence of the tariffs is in question, given that it's a question for the Supreme Court in the USA), impacts here could be significant. Even with perhaps more clarity here, I believe this still poses a significant risk. Even with the tariffs as a risk, I view the company to be attractive well beyond its current price, with a significant upside potential.

I maintain that the company is a "BUY" here and attractive. I also maintain that it fulfills all of my investment criteria and is therefore worth your attention.

Thesis

Carl Zeiss Meditec is a qualitative medtech business originating in Germany but with an international growth and sales profile. It does not offer a compelling yield, but what it does offer is a decently compelling overall long-term upside, even after the 2025A that I am presenting here. Even conservatively speaking, if you accept some of the premiumization here, you have triple-digit upside potential, with 35% and potentially as high as 40% per year as of the end of the year 2027, if you're willing to put the company above 30x P/E. For safety, I would probably limit myself to about 30x - but higher returns have been possible historically, and technically still are.

As mentioned in previous pieces, these assumptions require you to accept a very decent premium. Nonetheless, I continue to say "buy" (a strong one now) when it comes to this company. A quality company has the right to cost quite a bit - and this is the highest I accept for a business like this. I believe the market is severely undervaluing the upside potential of this stock.

My fair value target is now, as of September 2025, a 30x-33x forward P/E, potentially and realistically upwards of 36x. The investment PT is now at €85/share, for a 15%+ annualized upside to this fair value target. I maintain my rating to "Strong Buy" as of this article, and I don't change my PT, despite the lower share price.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative, and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 2 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 5/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a valuation target of €85/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Strong Buy" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: WEEK: Yield From Weekly Distributions
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Investors seeking exposure to the treasury bill market have several options for doing so in an indexed manner. The Roundhill Weekly T-Bill ETF (WEEK) is one such option, with the particular feature of offering optimization of the payment schedule for the bills it purchases. As the name of the ETF suggests, the fund sacrifices maximization of returns in order to provide weekly payments to its investors, thereby optimizing the aforementioned payment schedule. It is important to make this clear before moving on to the thesis of this analysis, as it will depend greatly on the purposes of each investor. Readers should consider how they manage their liquidity and what their purposes are in managing their cash flow.

As we will see later, the US government is financing itself largely through Treasury bills (short-term bonds). If the government issues more supply, this means that bills become marginally cheaper and short-term yields rise. This is positive for funds that trade in bills, as they are short-term instruments and their interest income (carry) adjusts quickly. As regular readers will know, I generally recommend managing surplus liquidity by investing in this type of short-term instrument. For investors who value weekly cash flow, I recommend buying this vehicle, as there are no other market alternatives that settle carry on a weekly basis (we will look at the drawbacks later). If your goal is to maximize the return on that invested liquidity, then I recommend selling the vehicle and exposing yourself to other alternatives that are more suitable for that purpose, which we will analyze below.

ETF Overview

Looking at the main characteristics of this ETF, we find that it has a 30-day security yield of 3.58% with weekly distribution and a distribution ratio of 3.51%. This in itself does not tell us much, but if we compare it with the current yield on 3-month Treasury bills (see image below), we see that the difference is 0.13% in terms of the distribution rate. Here we can see the effect of the fund's fees (expense ratio of 0.19%) and the bid/ask spread of 0.03%.

If we look at the fund's holdings, we see that all of its assets are invested in treasury bills with different maturities. Each bill in its portfolio has practically the same weight (7.60% - 7.80%).

The Peers

When analyzing the drawbacks of WEEK and proposing some alternatives for investors seeking weekly income distribution, we will focus on WEEK's most similar peers in the market. For this comparison, I have selected the ETFs SGOV, BIL, USFR, and TFLO (the latter two do not purchase bills, but rather have FRNs attached to short-term rates in their portfolios). As can be seen in the first image, in terms of fees, WEEK does not compete with SGOV or BIL (funds in the same category). These have annual expense ratios of 0.09% and 0.14%, respectively. As I said earlier, if you want weekly payments, buy WEEK, but if that doesn't matter so much to you, I would stick with SGOV to park liquidity. In terms of AUM, we can see that WEEK has significantly lower assets under management than SGOV or BIL. This may directly influence a wider bid-ask spread in the case of WEEK, with less market depth.

When we look at the images of price and total returns, we see the following. WEEK is much less “volatile” than other ETFs and performs worse in terms of total returns. 3.47% vs. 4.21% for SGOV. This shows that WEEK's weekly payment management sacrifices some performance. With a higher cost, I still recommend SGOV, if you don't mind not receiving those weekly flows.

T- Bill Treasury Management

Finally, I will focus on the more macroeconomic part of the article and on the statement I have made about why I prefer Treasury bills for managing short-term liquidity. The two images above show the percentage of US sovereign debt issuance relative to the country's GDP throughout the 21st century (first image) and the total percentage of debt issuance by instrument (image 2). As the reader can see, the US Treasury has been issuing Treasury bills throughout all these years until it is now financed mostly by this type of short-term instrument. Specifically, in 2025, most of the issuance by maturity is bills. This directly influences the Treasury bills themselves, lowering their prices and raising their yields. If we add to this a Treasury and a Federal Reserve as structural demanders of bills ( see RMP program), we can say that a Treasury loaded with bills tends to sustain attractive yields at the front end as long as the Fed does not cut aggressively. That is why I like to manage my liquidity with Treasury bills in this macroeconomic environment of slow rate cuts. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Reviewing All My Stocks - December Dividend Income Report
data.description:
In 2016, I made a life-changing decision: I took a sabbatical, put my family in a small RV, and we drove all the way to Costa Rica.

Upon my return in 2017, I officially quit my job as a private banker at National Bank and started working full-time on my baby: Dividend Stocks Rock. I also decided to manage my pension account held at the National Bank. I’ve built and managed this portfolio publicly since 2017 to create and track a real-life case study.

In August 2017, I received $108,760.02 in a locked retirement account. Locked means I can’t add capital to the account, and growth is only generated through capital gains and dividends. I don’t report this portfolio’s results to brag about my returns or to suggest you follow my lead. My purpose was solely to share with our members how I manage my portfolio with all the good and the bad that inevitably takes place each month. I hope you have learned and will continue to learn from my experiences managing this portfolio.

Reviewing All My Stocks 2026

New year, new me, but same old dividend growth strategy! At the start of the year, I thought I would share my expectations and perceptions for each of my pension plan holdings. I significantly underperformed the market in 2025 (7.34% vs. 20.50% for my benchmark). However, it means little as I don’t invest for 12 months, but for 40 years. Since inception, I’m still on track to match the market while not chasing returns. I prefer to have control over my portfolio and be fully confident in my holdings instead of wondering how Mr. Market will react to waking up the next morning. Let’s review each position!

But first, the results!

Performance in Review

Let’s start with the numbers as of January 5th, 2025 (before the bell):

Original amount invested in September 2017 (no additional capital added): $108,760.02.

  • Current portfolio value: $324,300.60
  • Dividends paid: $5,227.59 (TTM)
  • Average yield: 1.61%
  • 2025 performance: +7.34%
  • VFV.TO= +12.18%, XIU.TO = +28.88%
  • Dividend growth: +1.5%

Total return since inception (Sep 2017-November 2025): +198.18%

Annualized return (98 months): 14.31%

Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (VFV:CA) annualized return (since Sept 2017): 16.23% (total return 241.80%)

iShares S&P/TSX 60 ETF (XIU:CA) annualized return (since Sept 2017): 12.84% (total return 168.30%)

Dynamic sector allocation calculated by DSR PRO as of January 5th, 2026 (before the bell).

Automatic Data Processing (ADP) – Industrial

ADP is still my favorite “quiet compounding machine”. Payroll is sticky, and the real magic is the mountain of recurring fees that keeps showing up like clockwork. What I expect going forward is steady mid-single-digit growth with margin discipline, plus the usual upside when employment stays healthier than the headlines suggest. The main thing I’m watching is wage and hiring momentum because ADP can feel it in the numbers before economists do. I don’t mind ADP’s poor short-term performance as the dividend triangle metrics are stellar.

Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD:CA) – Consumer Staples

Couche-Tard keeps doing what Couche-Tard does. It turns boring convenience retail sales into a cash engine with discipline and scale. What I expect is continued resilience in margins and cash generation, with management staying opportunistic on tuck-in deals while keeping the balance sheet in check. Let’s be honest, as they are long overdue for announcing a major acquisition! The big swing factor is fuel spreads and FX noise, but the long game is still about traffic, merchandise, and relentless execution.

Broadcom (AVGO) – Information Technology

Broadcom is the newest addition to my portfolio. I’ve been praising this company for 10 years (since 2016) so it’s about time I got a piece of the cake! I love AVGO’s proven growth-by-acquisition strategy. The company enjoys a steady income flow from its RF filter sales (improving smartphone connections), and it has surfed on the AI tailwinds as its chips facilitate faster and less energy-consuming calculations. Even if AI doesn’t turn out into this well of profitability expected by many, everyone needs more efficient computers.

Brookfield Renewable Corp (BEPC:CA) – Utilities

Brookfield Renewable is where I go for the long runway, contracted cash flows plus a developer’s mindset with management that thinks in decades. Ironically, I share the same mindset when it comes to investing, patient investors win. BEPC should continue to thrive if AI is at the center of this bullish narrative. However, I don’t fancy myself thinking price swings are over!

Brookfield Corp (BN:CA) – Financial Services

Brookfield is my “financial octopus” with lots of arms, lots of fee streams, and a talent for buying when others are nervous. What I expect is more focus on fundraising, fee-related earnings, and disciplined deployments tied to power, data, and real assets, especially where AI infrastructure needs real-world shovels. The company owns the entire infrastructure behind the energy demand from the rise of AI. BN owns power plants, renewable energy, power grids, pipelines, real estate, data center and cloud services. They are well-positioned to ride this wave. The stock can trade like a mood ring, but the business is built for patient compounding.

CCL Industries (CCL.B:CA) – Basic Materials

CCL is the kind of company that makes money on things you touch every day, like labels, packaging, and specialty materials, with a surprisingly global footprint. What I expect is continued steady execution and selective bolt-on deals with earnings driven more by operational discipline than heroic forecasts. The risk is that it can feel cyclical when industrial demand cools, even if the long-term story stays intact.

Dollarama (DOL:CA) – Consumer Discretionary

Dollarama is my “inflation pressure valve” stock. When households feel squeezed, the value retail outlet becomes a habit. What I expect is continued strong traffic and same-store sales, plus a long, slow integration story in Australia that will not be linear. DOL is executing its flawless playbook when it comes to expansion: opening a determined number of stores each year (now in Canada, Mexico, Central America and Australia!). The organic growth should continue along the same line. The biggest risk is simply lapping tough comparables and any margin noise from freight or wage pressure, but the model has proven it can adapt.

Fortis (FTS:CA) – Utilities

Fortis is still the portfolio’s seatbelt with regulated assets, visible capex, and a dividend policy that’s designed to be boring in the best way. I like to say that I can put FTS in a black box and open it in 10 years without any worry. What I expect is steady rate base growth and more updates around the multi-year capital plan with modest earnings growth doing the heavy lifting. The main risk is rate sensitivity as utilities can get tossed around when bond yields jump even if the fundamentals barely move.

Granite Real Estate Investment Trust (GRT.UN:CA) – REITs

Granite is my “frustrating investment with good numbers”. At least GRT had a good year in 2025! Nonetheless, I’m roughly breaking even on this investment while my total portfolio is almost up 300%… The REIT owns high quality industrial real estate with a management team that leans conservative. What I expect is continued emphasis on occupancy and rent spreads plus steady distributions that reflect the underlying cash flow discipline. The key risk remains refinancing and cap rates, but Granite has been actively managing through the rate regime.

Home Depot (HD) – Consumer Discretionary

Home Depot is the housing cycle thermometer, and right now that thermometer is reading “slow turnover, cautious homeowners.” What I expect is a gradual thaw as rates eventually ease and projects come back, with Pros and repair and maintenance doing more of the work than big remodel dreams. 2026 should be a better year for the world’s largest home retailer. The risk is that the housing slump lasts longer than anyone wants, and HD can look stuck in place for a while, even if it’s building future demand quietly.

LeMaitre Vascular (LMAT) – Healthcare

LeMaitre is one of my favorite small compounders. It’s not flashy, but it keeps shipping specialized vascular tools into a growing market. I like the niche of selling equipment to surgeons and the robust balance sheet along with double-digit dividend increases! What I expect is continued organic growth with strong profitability, and management staying disciplined on product focus rather than chasing shiny distractions. The risk is always hospital purchasing cycles and procedure volumes, but the recent margin profile shows how powerful the niche can be when the execution is tight.

Microsoft (MSFT) – Information Technology

Microsoft is still the empire of recurring revenue, and AI is becoming an add-on engine across the whole stack. I first bought it for its subscription-based business and its cloud opportunity (Azure). Now the cloud is growing even faster thanks to AI! What I expect is continued Azure momentum and Copilot adoption, with near-term margin pressure as they keep investing heavily in AI infrastructure. The risk is that “capacity and spend” headlines spook the market, but long term, this is exactly how moats get deeper.

National Bank (NA:CA) – Financial Services

National Bank is my favorite Canadian bank. The bank is not just my favorite because I worked there for 13 years, but because it constantly reports strong numbers. It’s always in the top 2 or 3 among the Big 6 in all metrics (revenue growth, EPS growth, dividend growth, dividend payout ratio). The bank acquired Canadian Western Bank in early 2025 and ended the year by acquiring Laurentian Bank’s banking book of business. I expect another year of flawless execution.

Royal Bank (RY:CA) – Financial Services

RBC is Canada’s largest bank and my second favorite behind NA. I like the size, the scale and the diversification (50% of its revenue doesn’t come from classic banking activities). What I expect is continued strength in wealth and capital markets plus the usual focus on credit quality as the economy digests higher rates. The risk is not one bad quarter. It’s the slow creep of credit costs if unemployment rises, but RBC has the balance sheet and earnings power to manage cycles.

Stella-Jones (SJ:CA) – Basic Materials

Stella-Jones is the kind of business that looks dull on the surface, but then you realize rail ties and utility poles are long-lived necessities. What I expect is steady demand in the core categories with pricing and project timing doing most of the quarter-to-quarter dance. The risk is that volume can soften in parts of the cycle, but the “infrastructure replacement” drumbeat usually keeps playing.

Toromont Industries (TIH:CA) – Industrial

Toromont is my “Canada builds things” proxy, and I like having that exposure when the industrial cycle cooperates. For those expecting like me that more investment in infrastructure is coming, TIH should be in a great position to surf this tailwind. What I expect is continued strength from CIMCO and solid product support with equipment sales more dependent on mining and construction timing. The risk is a choppier capex environment, but the aftermarket and service side helps smooth the ride.

Visa Inc (V) – Financial Services

Visa is the toll booth on global commerce, and toll booths do not need to be exciting to be insanely profitable. What I expect is continued growth in payments volume and cross border activity with pricing power showing up through the cycle. The risk is regulatory noise and occasional macro slowdowns, but the cashless trend keeps pushing the river in Visa’s direction.

Waste Connections (WCN:CA) – Industrials

Waste Connections is my “boring, beautiful monopoly by route density”, once you own the pickup routes, the economics get very sticky. What I expect is steady revenue growth, disciplined pricing, and continued shareholder returns, including the dividend growth they’ve been showing. The risk is acquisition integration and commodity volatility around recycling, but the core hauling business tends to be remarkably dependable.

Smith Manoeuvre Update

Slowly but surely, the portfolio is taking shape with 12 companies spread across 7 sectors. My goal is to build a portfolio of thriving companies with a solid dividend triangle (e.g., with positive revenue, EPS and dividend growth trends). The current portfolio yield is at 2.23% with a 5-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 11.59%.

  • The portfolio value is now at $29,313.61
  • The portfolio debt is at $23,000.
  • Monthly contribution is set at $1,000/month.
  • The annual income is $653.60, and the projected income is $729.33
  • To report my Smith Manoeuvre, I export the Excel data from my DSR PRO dashboard.

Smith Manoeuvre Portfolio Summary

Here’s my SM portfolio summary as of January 5th, 2026 (before the bell).

$1,000 per month starting in January

I will start 2026 by adding $1,000 to some of my smallest positions. For example, I added $1,000 in Great-West (GWO:CA) with my January contribution.

The point will be to balance the portfolio and then move forward. I also have a few stocks in mind to add next year!

Pension Portfolio Summary

Here’s my pension plan portfolio summary as of January 5th, 2026 (before the bell).

Dividend Income: $770.14 (FLAT VS. DECEMBER 2024)

Since I started this portfolio in September 2017, I have received a total of $34,439.59 CAD in dividends. Keep in mind that this is a “pure dividend growth portfolio” as no capital can be added to this account other than retained and/or reinvested dividends. Therefore, all dividend growth is coming from the stocks and not from any additional capital being added to the account.

Final Thoughts

I’m starting strong in 2026 with the increase of my monthly contribution to my Smith Manoeuvre account. I already have over $1,000 in cash in my pension account, thanks to several dividend payments. I’ll be looking to buy more income-producing assets in January!

Original Post
